econSignals.label: Fastest Growing Sector: ICT Freelancing & Digital Services
econSignals.description: Freelance and digital services employment grew at an estimated 8.7% annually over 2018–2023 — the fastest-expanding entry path for skilled youth in urban Bangladesh.

econSignals.label: Second: RMG & Garments
econSignals.description: Ready-made garments remains a major employer with resilient export demand; employment growth is slower than ICT but still positive at an estimated 4.1% annually.

econSignals.label: Median Monthly Earnings — ICT / Freelancing
econSignals.description: ICT and freelancing workers in Dhaka and Chittagong report typical monthly earnings near $210 USD median, with a wide range ($120–$480) by platform and skill.

econSignals.label: Median Monthly Earnings — RMG (Garments)
econSignals.description: Garment sector earnings cluster around the statutory floor with median take-home near $95 USD/month; observed range about $75–$130 with overtime.

econSignals.label: Returns to Upper Secondary Education (HSC)
econSignals.source: World Bank Human Capital Index / WDI
econSignals.description: HSC-level workers in Bangladesh earn 32% more than JSC-level workers on average. Polytechnic diploma adds a further 28% over HSC in manufacturing and construction.

econSignals.label: Youth NEET Rate (not in education, employment, or training)
econSignals.description: Roughly three in ten Bangladeshi youth 15–24 are NEET — a large pool of unmapped skills if credentialing and matching stay informal.

econSignals.label: Formal Employment Share
econSignals.description: Only 14% of Bangladesh's workforce is formally employed. The remaining 86% are in informal self-employment, casual labor, or unregistered work.
